sensors.description: Report-only. Reads .claude/state/sensor-outcomes.jsonl + GATE_CATALOG and names never-fired / never-blocked commit gates so /retro can propose retiring them. Degrades loudly (INSUFFICIENT DATA) below --min-runs. Run via `npm run sensor-value` or in /retro.

sensors.signal: an interface contract crosses an ownership-cluster boundary with no story able to publish it
sensors.description: Ownership-cluster planner (gap G37): dependency-graph.json groups stories by topological DEPTH, which answers 'what can be scheduled next' — orthogonal to 'what can one engineer own end to end'. A depth level mixes unrelated subsystems into one branch while a vertical slice spans three levels, so the wave graph cannot be used to allocate work across a team. story-clusters.js computes the ownership view from specs/stories/stories.json: connected components over HARD edges only (data|behavior — the consumer needs the producer's runtime effect), with contract|ui edges treated as cuttable because publishing the interface first lets both engineers work at once. Oversized components split on the point-balancing bridge; a biconnected blob is reported oversized rather than split on an invented boundary. Undersized components merge into the peer they share the most cut edges with. Emits specs/stories/story-clusters.json (clusters with coordination_cost + independently_startable, interface_contracts naming the contract_story that publishes each artifact, blocking_dependencies for hard edges that had to be cut) and specs/stories/dependency-edges.json. BLOCKS (/spec Step 4.5, npm run story-clusters) when an interface contract has contract_story: null — the cut is fiction, the consumer must wait for the producer's full implementation, and allocating those clusters to separate engineers would surface a hidden serial dependency mid-sprint. Cutting a hard edge is never presented as parallelism: the downstream cluster is marked independently_startable: false. Waves are untouched — wave-plan.js, --pod, and per-cluster PRs keep their existing semantics.
sensors.net_add_justification: No existing control answers who can own what. dependency-graph.json + wave-plan.js answer scheduling order only, and their groups are routinely mistaken for work packages — a depth level mixes unrelated subsystems into one branch while a vertical slice is torn across three. Allocating from that graph produces hidden serial dependencies discovered mid-sprint. This is the first control that measures ownership independence rather than asserting it, and it is a hard gate precisely because the failure is silent: everything looks parallel until an engineer blocks. It adds no runtime cost to build lanes (planning cadence only) and leaves the wave machinery untouched.

sensors.signal: a requirement-taxonomy slot is neither covered by a requirement nor excused with a substantive reason
sensors.description: BRD comprehensiveness floor (gap G38): grounding-check.js proves the BRD invented and dropped nothing RELATIVE TO ITS SOURCE, but cannot prove the source asked the right questions — if the FRD never mentions retention, authz, or failure modes, the BRD is silent too and every existing gate still passes, leaving 'comprehensive' to mean only 'all sections non-empty'. brd-taxonomy-check.js asserts a fixed ten-slot floor (functional, data_lifecycle, integration, performance, security_authz, privacy_retention, observability, operability_failure, ux_accessibility, constraints): each slot needs a BR requirement tagged with it, or an entry in specs/brd/taxonomy-coverage.json giving a substantive na_reason. Placeholder excuses ('N/A', 'none', 'TBD', anything under 25 chars) are rejected — the gate exists to force the question to be asked, and a box-tick means it was not; the reason is committed so a reviewer can disagree with it. Also catches untagged requirements and misspelled slot names rather than silently ignoring them, and fails loudly on an empty spine instead of passing vacuously. BLOCKS at /brd Step 4.45 before the rubric runs; the rubric's completeness criterion is anchored to the verdict instead of judging prose.
sensors.net_add_justification: Nothing else in the harness can catch an unasked question. Every traceability control is relative — grounding, trace-check, and the AC round-trip all verify that downstream matches upstream, so a gap present in the FRD propagates cleanly through all of them and arrives as a production defect in retention, authz, or failure handling. This is the only absolute check in the planning chain, it is cheap (planning cadence, deterministic, no runtime cost), and its failure mode is the one the harness has repeatedly been burned by: a green pipeline over a silently incomplete spec.

drift_sensors_planned.note: The 'drift' cadence is the article's third column (repeatedly, slower, outside the change lifecycle). The architecture / dead-code / dependency-CVE signals are now SHIPPED via drift-report.js (see the drift-* sensors above). The runtime-SLO signal is now covered by the 'runtime-slo' integration sensor (slo-check.js): it ships as an integration-cadence sensor wired into /evaluate Step P4, and is also runnable standalone on a drift cadence (npm run slo -- --url <live>). No drift signals remain planned.

existing_drift_like.note: Watches the harness's OWN upstream (anthropics/claude-code), not the product codebase.
